metadata.summary: Make debt visible, quantify interest cost, prioritize paydown against roadmap. Allocate 15-20% sprint capacity. Score on Interest/Contagion/Effort/Alignment.

content.title: Technical Debt Management
content.summary: Systematic process for making technical debt visible, quantifying its interest cost, prioritizing paydown against the roadmap, and allocating 15-20% of sprint capacity continuously. Classifies debt into 6 types (deliberate, accidental, bit-rot, design, documentation, test), scores each on Interest/Contagion/Effort/Alignment, and sequences paydown aligned with planned feature work to minimize marginal cost.

why.p: Technical debt is invisible to non-technical stakeholders and never systematically addressed until it causes a crisis. The interest metaphor makes the cost concrete: debt slows delivery by a measurable fraction of sprint velocity, compounds over time, and blocks future features. Making it visible with a register and quantifying it in dev-days-per-quarter creates the business case needed to allocate capacity consistently.

list.item: Codebase has aging dependencies, fragmented patterns, and "fear zones" engineers avoid.
Onboarding: agent surveys repo for hot-spots and surfaces undocumented assumptions.
Quarterly debt-paydown planning: scoring and sequencing the register against upcoming roadmap.
Pre-acquisition or pre-investment due diligence: producing defensible debt inventory.
Migration planning (lib upgrade, framework jump, monolith decomposition).
One-person codebase under 6 months old — debt is rounding error, ship features.
Debt that is actually a missing feature — do not classify "we never built X" as "we built X badly".
Production incident response — debt management is a planning activity, not firefighting.
Code-quality theater — do not run an agent to generate a debt list nobody will fund.
Deliberate — conscious shortcut: "Ship now, refactor later"
Accidental — learned better way after the fact: "Didn't know about X pattern"
Bit-rot — environment changed: dependencies outdated, platform APIs deprecated
Design — architecture limitations: "Can't scale current approach"
Documentation — missing docs: "No one knows how this works"
Test — missing tests: "Can't safely change X without coverage"

rule.statement: Distinguish Prudent Deliberate debt (conscious shortcut with plan to fix) from Reckless debt (no design awareness). Only prudent debt is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Reckless debt — "no time for design" or "what's a design?" — compounds without a paydown trigger. Prudent deliberate debt has a known cost and a plan.

rule.statement: Test debt is not optional: untested code cannot be safely refactored. Prioritize test debt before structural refactoring.
rule.rationale: Refactoring untested code produces regressions that are invisible until production. Tests are the safety net that makes all other paydown possible.

rule.statement: Score each debt item on four factors (1-5 each): Interest (how much it slows velocity), Contagion (does it spread?), Effort (cost to fix), Alignment (proximity to planned work). Priority = (Interest x Alignment) / Effort.
rule.rationale: Interest × Alignment / Effort identifies debt that is hurting us now AND can be fixed cheaply because we're already touching that code. This is the lowest marginal-cost paydown strategy.

antipattern.description: Agent scoring style/lint issues as "design debt".
antipattern.reason: Style and formatting are cosmetic, not structural. Inflating the register with cosmetic items buries real debt. Apply a severity rubric and reject purely stylistic signals at the classification stage.

antipattern.description: Using LLM-generated time estimates ("2 days") without team calibration.
antipattern.reason: LLM effort estimates for debt paydown are confabulation; actual work is 5-10x more. Calibrate from past resolved-debt items in the register.

rule.statement: Allocate 15-20% of sprint capacity continuously to debt paydown rather than saving it for "debt sprints".
rule.rationale: Debt sprints allow debt to re-accumulate between them. Sustained 15-20% allocation keeps the interest rate stable and builds the habit.

list.item: Dedicated % (recommended) — 15-20% of each sprint, continuous
Boy Scout — clean as you touch, 30-min cap per PR to prevent scope creep
Debt sprints — quarterly focused sprint, only for large architectural work
Spike — specific investigation of an unknown, before adding to register
Step 1: Make visible — tag items in tracker, keep a register, add code comments (TODO/FIXME with ticket links)
Step 2: Quantify — measure time tax (dev-days slowed per quarter), risk (what could break), opportunity cost (what features are blocked)
Step 3: Prioritize — score on Interest/Contagion/Effort/Alignment, rank
Step 4: Allocate — 15-20% continuous + quarterly focused time for architectural work
Step 5: Pay down strategically — fix during related feature work, fix highest-interest first, fix blocking debt first
Step 6: Prevent new debt — Definition of Done includes "no new debt added", code review catches it

rule.statement: Make debt visible with money, not adjectives: "this costs 6 dev-days per quarter" beats "this is bad code".
rule.rationale: Non-technical stakeholders cannot prioritize "bad code" against features. Quantified business impact creates the case for allocation.

rule.statement: Every new reckless debt item added to the register must link to an Architectural Decision Record explaining why it was accepted.
rule.rationale: Debt without a decision record has no paydown trigger. ADR creates accountability and makes the cost explicit to whoever incurred it.

list.item: repo-surveyor (sonnet) — runs static analysis tools, assembles raw signals (churn, complexity, TODOs, stale deps)
debt-classifier (sonnet) — reviews signals + PR comments, classifies into 6 types, scores 4 factors
debt-prioritizer (opus, quarterly) — intersects scored register with roadmap, proposes paydown sequence
boy-scout-reviewer (haiku, per-PR) — inline cleanup suggestions with 30-min scope cap

rule.statement: Apply debt paydown changes via PR; never auto-merge debt-paydown commits.
rule.rationale: Debt fixes change production behavior. Auto-merged paydown bypasses review and can introduce regressions or break consumers of refactored APIs.

antipattern.description: Agent proposing big-bang rewrites as the "fix approach" for debt items.
antipattern.reason: LLMs bias toward rewrites. Prompt constraint: minimal local change, avoid diffs over 20 lines without explicit justification.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-upgrading dependencies with semver-major version bumps.
antipattern.reason: Breaking changes in major upgrades require consumer testing. Route to humans and require breaking-change detection before any major upgrade is applied.

rule.statement: Paydown capacity allocation (the % of sprint dedicated to debt) is engineering-leadership territory. Agents propose; humans approve.
rule.rationale: Changing the allocation ratio affects feature velocity visible to stakeholders. It requires trade-off authority that agents do not have.

list.item: scc / tokei — LOC + complexity counts
radon (Python) — Cyclomatic, maintainability metrics
lizard — Multi-language complexity analysis
semgrep — Pattern + security findings
dependency-cruiser (JS) — Import graph, cycle detection
pydeps (Python) — Module graph analysis
pip-audit / npm audit / osv-scanner — Dependency vulnerability + outdated checks
git-of-theseus — Code aging analysis
code-maat — Hot-spot mining via git history
SonarQube / SonarCloud — Severity-graded findings; CI integration
CodeScene — Hot-spot + behavioral analysis
Codacy / Code Climate — Multi-language quality grades
Snyk / Dependabot / Renovate — Bot-driven dependency upgrades
Trunk.io — Aggregates linters; agent-friendly artifacts
GitHub Code Scanning — Free for OSS; agent reads JSON output
Stepsize — Debt tracking integrated with Jira/Linear/GitHub

section.name: common-ai-agent-failures
section.p: Agents love rewrites; bias toward big-bang refactors which are wrong. Prompt: "minimal local change; avoid >20-line diffs without reason". Style nits flagged as "design debt" inflate the register. Filter at signal stage. Generated "fix approaches" hallucinate APIs/libraries. Verify with build. Cyclomatic complexity is not always debt — sometimes inherent. Force comparison vs team baseline. Estimates are confabulation: agent says "2 days"; actual is 2 weeks. Calibrate via past resolved items.

Dependency upgrade auto-merges break consumers; route to humans and require breaking-change detection. Long-context whole-repo scans cost compute; tier the analysis (cheap signals → mid → deep dive only on top-20 hot files). Agents conflate "old code" with "bad code"; old + working + low-churn = leave alone.

list.item: Make debt visible with money, not adjectives: "this slows us 6 dev-days/quarter" beats "this is bad code".
Pay down where you're already touching: lowest marginal cost. Pair debt items with feature work.
Boy-scout rule with limits: 30-min cap per PR; agent flags scope creep.
15-20% sustained allocation > debt sprints: sprint approach lets debt re-accumulate between them.
Track trend, not absolute: total debt count is meaningless; high-interest count + interest trend matter.
Reckless debt requires architectural decision record (ADR): agent should refuse to file new debt without an ADR link.
Test debt isn't optional: untested code can't be refactored safely; prioritize test debt before structural.
Bit-rot has a clock: dependency upgrades compound — Renovate/Dependabot weekly.
Don't refactor working code without a reason: agent must tie every refactor to a feature/bug/risk.
LLMs love rewrites; bias toward big-bang refactors which are exactly the wrong move. Prompt: "minimal local change; avoid >20-line diffs without reason".
Style nits flagged as "design debt" inflate the register. Filter at signal stage, not rationalization stage.
Generated "fix approaches" routinely hallucinate APIs/libraries. Verify with build before committing.
Cyclomatic complexity ≠ debt — sometimes inherent. Force comparison vs. team baseline.
Estimates are confabulation: agent says "2 days"; actual is 2 weeks. Calibrate via past resolved-debt items.
Dependency upgrade auto-merges break consumers; pin "breaking change" detection (semver-major) and route to humans.
Long-context whole-repo scans cost compute; tier the analysis (cheap signals → mid → deep dive only on top-20 hot files).
Agents conflate "old code" with "bad code"; old + working + low-churn = leave alone.

template.purpose: Per-item register: type, location, description, impact (time tax + risk), fix effort, priority, related work.
Quarterly scoring table with Interest/Contagion/Effort/Alignment columns, sprint plan, and deferred list.
Sprint-level debt work log: budget, items, effort, owners, outcomes.

script.purpose: Quarterly repo survey: git churn, radon complexity, TODO/FIXME, outdated deps, coverage gaps → feeds debt-classifier agent.
